cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer to the question about the headline "Safina goes from basket case to top of the world." The user wants to know what we can infer from it based on the provided passage. First, I need to understand the context of the headline in the article. The article mentions that the Sydney Morning Herald used this headline during the Australian Open. The passage earlier talks about Safina's emotional volatility, comparing her to her brother Marat Safin, who was known for his temper. The article also says that a newspaper once called her "mad as a snake," which suggests she's had emotional ups and downs during her matches. The headline uses the phrase "basket case," which is an idiom. A basket case usually refers to someone who is very nervous, anxious, or emotionally unstable. So "from basket case to top of the world" would imply a transformation from being someone who was struggling emotionally or under a lot of stress to achieving a great success, like becoming number one in the world rankings. Looking at the options: A) Safina wanted to top the world when she was a baby in a basket. This seems unlikely because "basket case" isn't about a baby in a basket. It's an idiom meaning someone who's a mess emotionally. B) After she won this year's Australian Open, Dinara Safina cried. The headline doesn't mention crying; it's about emotional transformation, not necessarily crying after a win. C) Too nervous at first, but Safina finally gained confidence and won. This fits because "basket case" could refer to her initial nervousness or emotional instability, and "top of the world" refers to her success. The article mentions she has made it to finals and won a silver medal, showing her progress. D) She learned from the basket
